proposed_short: Whether an action is viewed as complete (perfective) or as ongoing / repeated / habitual (imperfective). Italian's imperfect–passato prossimo contrast is fundamentally aspectual.
proposed_long: Aspect is a separate axis from tense. Tense locates an event in time (past, present, future); aspect describes how the event is viewed: as a finished and bounded whole, as something in progress, as something repeated, or simply as a state with no particular endpoints. Italian's two main past tenses split on aspect rather than on time-distance: the passato prossimo presents a past event as complete, the imperfect presents it as ongoing, habitual, or backgrounded. English marks aspect less systematically (the past progressive 'was walking' is overt, the habitual reading of 'used to walk' is lexical, and the simple past 'walked' is aspectually neutral), which is why English-speaking learners often have to acquire the imperfect–PP contrast as a new analytic distinction rather than a translation rule.
rationale: Used in usage and discrimination explanations across the imperfect topic; 'aspectual contrast' is the standard short label for the imperfect–PP opposition.

proposed_short: An aspect that presents an action as in progress at the reference point: 'I was walking' / camminavo / stavo camminando.
proposed_long: Progressive aspect marks an action as unfolding at the reference time rather than as completed. Italian expresses it two ways: with the bare imperfect (camminavo, which is broad because it also covers the habitual reading 'used to walk') or with the periphrastic stare + gerund (stavo camminando, narrower and unambiguously 'was walking' at that moment). The bare imperfect is the workhorse; the periphrastic adds emphasis when the in-progress reading needs to be foregrounded.
rationale: Needed for usage.ongoing_descriptive and indirect_speech explanations, to distinguish progressive from habitual readings of the same imperfect form.

proposed_short: Reading a past-tense form as pointing toward something that was about to happen, rather than already in progress.
proposed_long: A prospective reading takes a verb form to refer not to an event already underway but to one yet to come from the reference time. In Italian's indirect speech, the imperfect carries a prospective reading when the embedded clause reports a future intention from the standpoint of a past reporting verb: 'Ha detto che andava al cinema' is most naturally read as 'he said he was going to go to the cinema' (about to happen), rather than 'he said he was going' (in progress). The periphrastic stare per + infinitive ('ha detto che stava per uscire' = 'he said he was about to leave') makes the prospective reading explicit.

proposed_short: Using the imperfect of volere (sometimes potere) to soften a present-time request: 'Volevo un caffè' = 'I'd like a coffee'.
proposed_long: The polite-present is a use of the imperfect indicative to express a present-time wish or request, where the distancing effect of the past tense makes the request feel less direct. 'Volevo un caffè' is more polite than 'voglio un caffè' because the imperfect places the wanting in the past, even though the actual want is current. Most common with volere ('volevo chiederti...' = 'I wanted to ask you...') and to a lesser extent potere. The present conditional (vorrei) carries similar softening and is interchangeable in many contexts; the polite-present imperfect is the colloquial alternative.

proposed_short: The 'if' clause of a conditional sentence. In 'Se avessi tempo, verrei', the protasis is 'Se avessi tempo'.
proposed_long: In a conditional sentence the protasis is the if-clause and the apodosis is the then-clause. Italian's hypothetical conditional (the periodo ipotetico della irrealtà) traditionally uses the imperfect subjunctive in the protasis with the present conditional in the apodosis: 'Se avessi tempo, verrei' = 'If I had time, I would come'. In colloquial Italian both clauses can collapse to plain imperfect indicative ('Se avevo tempo, venivo'), which is what the colloquial-counterfactual stylistic leaf flags.

proposed_short: The 'then' clause of a conditional sentence. In 'Se avessi tempo, verrei', the apodosis is 'verrei'.
proposed_long: Paired with protasis. The apodosis names the consequent of a conditional: what would happen, did happen, or would have happened given the if-clause. Italian's apodosis form depends on the conditional type (present conditional for present hypotheticals, past conditional for past hypotheticals, present indicative for real conditionals). In the colloquial counterfactual both protasis and apodosis collapse to imperfect indicative.

proposed_short: Italian's three-way classification of conditional sentences: della realtà (real), della possibilità (possible), della irrealtà (counterfactual).
proposed_long: Italian grammar describes conditional sentences in three classes. Periodo ipotetico della realtà: present indicative in both clauses for real-world possibilities ('Se piove, resto a casa'). Periodo ipotetico della possibilità: imperfect subjunctive in the protasis, present conditional in the apodosis, for hypothetical but possible situations ('Se piovesse, resterei a casa'). Periodo ipotetico della irrealtà: trapassato congiuntivo in the protasis, past conditional in the apodosis, for counterfactual situations ('Se fosse piovuto, sarei restato a casa'). The colloquial counterfactual collapses both clauses to imperfect indicative, regardless of class.
rationale: Needed in stylistic.colloquial_counterfactual to name the prescriptive constructions the colloquial form is substituting for.
